extra.reasoning: The property requires NOT(rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b). Only one rule sets rec_drug_a: [rec_a_from_conjunction, priority 50] IF prior_gi_bleed THEN rec_drug_a := true. Only one rule sets rec_drug_b: [rec_b_from_trigger, priority 50] IF prior_gi_bleed THEN rec_drug_b := true. Both rules share the identical trigger condition (prior_gi_bleed). The priority-based conflict resolution operates per output (the matching rule with the highest priority wins for that output), so priorities never prevent two different outputs from being set by rules that share a trigger. When prior_gi_bleed = true, rec_a_from_conjunction fires setting rec_drug_a := true and rec_b_from_trigger fires setting rec_drug_b := true simultaneously, making both outputs true. With peptic_ulcer = asthma = diabetes = false, rec_drug_c has no firing rule and defaults to false. Thus the assignment prior_gi_bleed=true, peptic_ulcer=false, asthma=false, diabetes=false yields rec_drug_a=true AND rec_drug_b=true, violating mutual exclusion. (The other input values are irrelevant; any input with prior_gi_bleed=true violates the property.)
